metadata.summary: Evaluate OpenAI fine-tuned models: LLM-as-judge scoring, A/B comparison, format compliance, failure analysis, and quality gates before production deployment.

content.title: OpenAI Fine-Tuning Evaluation
content.summary: Before routing production traffic to a fine-tuned model, evaluate it against the base model on a held-out test set using automated LLM-as-judge scoring and A/B comparison. Eval must pass quality gates on accuracy, format compliance, and human preference rate before deployment is approved.

why.p: Fine-tuned model behavior on out-of-distribution inputs is unpredictable; eval on real traffic samples (not just synthetic data) is required. A model that scores well on the training distribution can regress on edge cases or slightly different phrasings. The training loss curve is not a sufficient proxy for production quality — it measures fit to training data, not real-world utility.

Human checkpoint before routing live traffic to a new fine-tuned model is mandatory. Automated eval catches regressions; human review catches failures that automated metrics miss (tone, brand voice, safety).

list.item: After every fine-tuning job completes, before any production traffic is routed to the new model.
When deciding whether to increase epoch count or add more training data.
When comparing multiple fine-tuned model candidates (different hyperparameters, different data versions).
Periodic regression testing after retraining on updated datasets.
Evaluating base models without fine-tuning context — use standard benchmarks instead.
Replacing human review entirely — automated eval catches metric regressions, not all failure modes.

section.name: quality-gates
section.p: Minimum quality gates before deploying a fine-tuned model to production:

list.item: Test accuracy above baseline (fine-tuned model must outperform base on the target task).
Human preference rate above 60% (prefer fine-tuned over base in blind comparison).
No regressions on held-out test cases from previous model versions.

rule.statement: Always hold out a separate test set (not used in training or validation) for final evaluation. Run both base model and fine-tuned model on the identical test set before comparing. Use temperature=0 for deterministic comparison.
rule.rationale: Training and validation splits assess fit; only a truly held-out test set measures generalization. Temperature=0 eliminates stochastic variance from the comparison, making differences attributable to the model rather than sampling noise.

section.name: llm-as-judge
section.p: Use GPT-4o as the evaluator model for automated scoring of fine-tuned model outputs. Score on a 1-5 scale with explicit rubric criteria.

example.title: ModelEvaluator — LLM-as-judge
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI from typing import List, Dict, Tuple from dataclasses import dataclass import json
@dataclass class EvalResult: score: float details: Dict examples: List[Dict]
class ModelEvaluator: """Evaluate fine-tuned model performance."""
def __init__( self, model_name: str, evaluator_model: str = "gpt-4o" ): self.client = OpenAI() self.model_name = model_name self.evaluator_model = evaluator_model
def evaluate( self, test_data: List[Dict], eval_prompt_template: str ) -> EvalResult: """Run evaluation on test data."""
scores = [] examples = []
for test_case in test_data: # Get model response response = self._get_model_response(test_case)
# Evaluate score, explanation = self._evaluate_response( test_case, response, eval_prompt_template )
scores.append(score) examples.append({ "input": test_case, "output": response, "score": score, "explanation": explanation })
avg_score = sum(scores) / len(scores) if scores else 0
return EvalResult( score=avg_score, details={ "total": len(test_data), "avg_score": avg_score, "min_score": min(scores) if scores else 0, "max_score": max(scores) if scores else 0, "distribution": {i: scores.count(i) for i in range(1, 6)} }, examples=examples )
def _get_model_response(self, test_case: Dict) -> str: messages = [ m for m in test_case["messages"] if m["role"] != "assistant" ]
response = self.client.chat.completions.create( model=self.model_name, messages=messages, temperature=0 )
return response.choices[0].message.content
def _evaluate_response( self, test_case: Dict, response: str, template: str ) -> Tuple[int, str]: expected = next( (m["content"] for m in test_case["messages"] if m["role"] == "assistant"), "" ) user_input = next( (m["content"] for m in test_case["messages"] if m["role"] == "user"), "" )
eval_prompt = template.format( input=user_input, expected=expected, actual=response )
eval_response = self.client.chat.completions.create( model=self.evaluator_model, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": eval_prompt}], response_format={"type": "json_object"}, temperature=0 )
result = json.loads(eval_response.choices[0].message.content) return result["score"], result.get("explanation", "")

section.name: ab-comparison
section.p: A/B comparison runs both base model and fine-tuned model on the same test queries and uses a judge model to determine the winner.

example.title: A/B model comparison
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI import json from typing import List, Dict
client = OpenAI()
def compare_models( base_model: str, fine_tuned_model: str, test_queries: List[str], system_prompt: str = "You are a helpful assistant." ) -> Dict: """A/B compare base vs fine-tuned model."""
comparisons = []
for query in test_queries: messages = [ {"role": "system", "content": system_prompt}, {"role": "user", "content": query} ]
# Get both responses base_response = client.chat.completions.create( model=base_model, messages=messages, temperature=0 )
ft_response = client.chat.completions.create( model=fine_tuned_model, messages=messages, temperature=0 )
base_output = base_response.choices[0].message.content ft_output = ft_response.choices[0].message.content
# Judge comparison judge_prompt = f"""Compare Response A and Response B for this query.
Query: {query}
Response A: {base_output}
Response B: {ft_output}
Evaluate both responses on: 1. Accuracy 2. Helpfulness 3. Clarity 4. Completeness
Return JSON: {{"winner": "A" or "B" or "tie", "a_score": 1-5, "b_score": 1-5, "reason": "Why the winner is better"}}"""
judgment = client.chat.completions.create( model="gpt-4o", messages=[{"role": "user", "content": judge_prompt}], response_format={"type": "json_object"}, temperature=0 )
result = json.loads(judgment.choices[0].message.content) comparisons.append({ "query": query, "base": base_output, "fine_tuned": ft_output, "winner": result["winner"], "reason": result["reason"] })
# Summarize wins = {"A": 0, "B": 0, "tie": 0} for c in comparisons: wins[c["winner"]] += 1
return { "comparisons": comparisons, "summary": { "base_wins": wins["A"], "fine_tuned_wins": wins["B"], "ties": wins["tie"], "fine_tuned_win_rate": wins["B"] / len(comparisons) } }
# Usage results = compare_models( base_model="gpt-4o-mini", fine_tuned_model="ft:gpt-4o-mini:my-org::abc123", test_queries=[ "How do I reset my password?", "What's your refund policy?", "The app isn't working" ] )
print(f"Fine-tuned win rate: {results['summary']['fine_tuned_win_rate']:.1%}")

section.name: eval-prompts
section.p: Standard evaluation prompts for automated quality assessment:

section.name: evaluation-methods
section.p: Automated evaluation methods by task type:

list.item: LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o): general quality, tone, helpfulness — use for open-ended responses.
Exact match: classification, entity extraction — binary correct/incorrect.
Format compliance: structured output (JSON schema validation, regex pattern matching).
ROUGE/BLEU: text generation with reference outputs — less reliable than LLM judge for quality.
Manual review: 20-50 sampled outputs for human preference judgment.

section.p: Model selection for evaluation tasks: use gpt-4o for quality and A/B evaluation; use gpt-4o-mini for bulk format compliance checks; use temperature=0 for all evaluation calls (deterministic).

antipattern.description: Evaluating only on the training distribution.
antipattern.reason: A fine-tuned model memorizes training patterns. Eval on a test set drawn from the same distribution as training overestimates production quality. Evaluate on real traffic samples or distribution-shifted examples to measure actual generalization.

antipattern.description: Using the fine-tuned model itself as the eval judge.
antipattern.reason: The fine-tuned model has learned biases from training data — it will score its own output style higher. Always use a different model (typically gpt-4o) as the judge to avoid circular evaluation.

antipattern.description: Skipping human review before production deployment.
antipattern.reason: Automated metrics catch metric-level regressions (accuracy, format, win rate). They miss subjective failures: inappropriate tone for brand context, subtle safety violations, outputs that are technically correct but user-hostile. Human checkpoint is mandatory before routing live traffic.
